steps.title: Understand the goal: refute the claim that urban districts are favored
steps.content: The student needs evidence that contradicts the idea that small urban districts benefit from malapportionment. The notes say the formula gives 1 point per resident but 1.8 points per unit of land — weighting land more heavily. The notes also directly state that rural districts (large land, low population) receive a disproportionate number of seats.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Awarding more points per unit of land than points per resident, the formula for distributing Storting seats overrepresents less populated rural districts with large tracts of land.': Uses the formula's specific weighting to show rural (not urban) districts are overrepresented — directly refuting the claim. ✓
  • Choice B — 'A common feature of representative governments, malapportionment occurs when electoral districts are over- or underrepresented.': Only defines malapportionment in general; says nothing about which districts benefit in the Storting. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Less populated rural districts are disproportionally underrepresented in the Storting, creating an unfair advantage for smaller but more populated urban districts.': Contradicts the notes — rural districts are overrepresented, not underrepresented. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It's untrue that malapportionment in the 169-seat Storting favors small urban districts; rather, the formula for distributing seats overrepresents more populated districts.': Correctly refutes the urban-favor claim but then says 'more populated' districts are overrepresented, which contradicts the notes (rural, less populated districts are overrepresented). ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
